<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوسو-إن]][[az:Şosoin]][[de:Shōsō-in]][[en:Shōsōin]][[fa:شوسواین]][[fr:Shōsō-in]][[hy:Սյոսոին]][[it:Shōsō-in]][[ja:正倉院]][[ko:쇼소인]][[nl:Shōsōin]][[pl:Shōsō-in]][[ru:Сёсоин]][[simple:Shōsōin]][[sl:Šoso-in]][[tr:Shōsōin]][[uk:Сьосоїн]][[zh:正仓院]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوسو-إن]][[az:Şosoin]][[de:Shōsō-in]][[en:Shōsōin]][[fa:شوسواین]][[fr:Shōsō-in]][[hy:Սյոսոին]][[it:Shōsō-in]][[ja:正倉院]][[ko:쇼소인]][[nl:Shōsōin]][[pl:Shōsō-in]][[ru:Сёсоин]][[simple:Shōsōin]][[sl:Šoso-in]][[tr:Shōsōin]][[uk:Сьосоїн]][[zh:正仓院]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوسو-إن]][[az:Şosoin]][[de:Shōsō-in]][[en:Shōsōin]][[fa:شوسواین]][[fr:Shōsō-in]][[hy:Սյոսոին]][[it:Shōsō-in]][[ja:正倉院]][[ko:쇼소인]][[nl:Shōsōin]][[pl:Shōsō-in]][[ru:Сёсоин]][[simple:Shōsōin]][[sl:Šoso-in]][[tr:Shōsōin]][[uk:Сьосоїн]][[zh:正仓院]]
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} [[File:Shoso-in.jpg|thumb|300px|Shōsō-in]] The {{nihongo|'''Shōsō-in'''|正倉院}} is the of [[Tōdai-ji]] Temple in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref name="shosoin">{{cite web|title=正倉院ホームページ (Shōsōin Homepage, Imperial Household Agency (Japan))|language= ja|url=http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/|access-date = 2007-03-12}}</ref><ref>"Shōsōin" originally stood for the warehouse area that many of the Buddhist temples and governmental sites in the ages of {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} were known to have, and {{nihongo|"Shōsō"|正倉}} was meaning each independent building located in such an area. However, all but the one in Tōdai-ji were lost over time, thus Shōsōin became a proper noun for the only remaining treasure house building at Tōdai-ji.</ref> The building is in the ''{{ill|Azekura-zukuri|en|Azekura-zukuri|WD=Q2875112|lt=Azekura-zukuri|1=Azekura-zukuri}}'' ([[:en:log-cabin|log-cabin]]) style with a raised floor. It lies to the northwest of the Great Buddha Hall. The Shōsō-in houses artifacts connected to {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} (聖武天皇)(701–756) and {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} (光明皇后)(701–760), as well as arts and crafts of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} (天平) era of {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=Japanese history|1=History of Japan}}.
== History == [[File:Kokka Chinpoh Choh Shosoin.JPG|thumb|Dedicatory records of Tōdai-ji temple, 756]] The construction of the [[Tōdai-ji]] Buddhist temple complex was ordained by {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} as part of a national project of Buddhist temple construction. During the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} period, the years during which {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} reigned, multiple disasters struck Japan as well as political uproar and epidemics. Because of these reasons {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} launched a project of provincial temples.<ref>{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple|1=Provincial temple}}</ref>{{Circular reference|date=April 2018}} The [[Tōdai-ji]] was appointed as the head temple of these provincial temples. {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} was a strong supporter of Buddhism and he thought it would strengthen his central authority as well. The origin of [[Tōdai-ji]]'s Shōsō-in repository itself dates back to 756, when {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} dedicated over 600 items to the Great Buddha at [[Tōdai-ji]] to express her love for her lost husband, Emperor Shōmu, who died 49 days earlier.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408">'Treasures in the Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi. Japan Quarterly, Oct 1, 1970, Vol.17(4), p.408.</ref> Her donation was made over five times across several years, then stored at the Shōsō-in. During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, a large number of treasures, consisting of items and instruments used in important Buddhist services were transferred from a different warehouse called the Kensakuin to the [[Tōdai-ji]].
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it came under the administration of the [[Government of Japan|national government]], and since {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} has been under the administration of the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. It is on the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} register of {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}s as one of the {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|WD=Q1055749|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara}}. It is also a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.
== Building == [[File:Azekura-dukuri JPN.JPG|thumb|''Azekura'' style of architecture on another store house at the Tōdai-ji]] [[File:正倉院 - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|Detail of the Shōsōin]] The building is in the [[:en:Azekurazukuri|Azekura Zukuri]] log-cabin style, with a floor raised to about 2.5 m {{nihongo|''takayuka-shiki''|高床式}}.<ref name="Shosoin 2009">'Shosoin : The oldest archive in Japan.' Library and archival security journal. Volume 22, 2009.</ref> This is an architectural style that was mainly used for the construction of {{ill|Granary|en|Granary|WD=Q114768|lt=granaries|1=Granary}} and storehouses. Some distinctive features of this building style are the triangular, wooden beams that come together in the corners, as well as the fact that it was assembled without using any bolts nor nails. This could be slightly surprising for its height of 14 m, width of 33 m and depth of about 9.3 m.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> However, it was a logical and smart step. As a result of assembling the storehouse without bolts or nails, the structure became very flexible and able to withstand earthquakes, a phenomenon of nature with which Japan was already well acquainted during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. The Shōsō-in is also the only building to survive the {{ill|Siege of Nara|en|Siege of Nara|WD=Q1116528|lt=Siege of Nara|1=Siege of Nara}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name=Turnbull3>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |isbn=0026205408 |page=50}}</ref> The exact construction date is unclear, but construction works probably started soon after the empress's bequest in AD 756 and definitely were finished before AD 759, when the bequest items storage lists were complete.
==Preservation of the artifacts== Since the Shōsō-in was to be a repository for (valuable) objects, it was constructed to create a natural climate regulation system. This natural climate regulation system was created by elevating the floor to a height of 2.7 m.<ref>'Shosoin' Japan Knowledge Lib, encyclopedia of Japan.</ref> This made circulation of air underneath the building possible and protected the structure against humidity at the same time. In addition to this, during the first few decades after its construction, the triangular beams of the Japanese cypress might have functioned as a natural regulator of humidity and temperature. The artefacts themselves were stored away in chests made from {{ill|Cedrus|en|Cedrus|WD=Q128550|lt=cedar|1=Cedrus}} wood, which is known for its durability.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> These chests were 90–110 cm long, 60–70 cm wide and 40–50 cm high. Not only the building itself but also these chests were elevated from the ground. All these adjustments made it possible to preserve the treasures in perfect state.
== Treasures == The Shōsō-in today holds around 9,000 items, excluding items that are yet to be classified.<ref>[https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385379 Piggott, Joan. (1990). "Mokkan. Wooden Documents from the Nara Period"], ''Monumenta Nipponica'', 45:4, pp. 449-470.</ref> The treasures that were donated by Empress Kōmyō were stored in the Hoku Sō, the Northern part of the Shōsōin. From the very beginning, this part of the Shōsō-in has been sealed by the imperial family. One was permitted to enter only with explicit permission of the imperial family.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408"/> Ninety-five percent of the fine arts and crafts in the Shōsōin were produced in Japan around the 8th century, with the remainder imported from the Tang Dynasty, Central Asia, India, Iran, etc. during the same period.<ref name = "narajoshi">[https://megalodon.jp/2020-0304-2055-57/www.nara-wu.ac.jp/liaison/lecture/20160827/ 公開講座『正倉院の工芸 遣唐使は何を持ちかえったか』] Nara Women's University</ref><ref name = "nishikawa">Akihiko Nishikawa. ''正倉院への道天平の至宝'' p.132. Yuzankaku Shuppan. {{ISBN|978-4639016434}}</ref> The designs of these Japanese and East Asian treasures show Iranian, Greek, Roman, and Egyptian influences due to cultural exchange via the Silk Road.<ref name = "narajoshi"/><ref name = "nishikawa"/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Eiland |first=Murray |date=1996 |title=The Ceramics of the Silk Route: Parthia and China |url=https://www.academia.edu/8013347 |journal=Transactions of the Oriental Ceramic Society |volume=60 |pages=105–120}}</ref>
Although these collections are not open to the public, selections are shown at {{ill|Nara National Museum|en|Nara National Museum|WD=Q147312|lt=Nara National Museum|1=Nara National Museum}} once a year in autumn.
The objects and treasures that have been stored in the Shōsō-in can be divided into the following categories. <ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/shisetsu/shosoin01.html |title=The Shosoin Repository - The Imperial Household Agency |publisher=Kunaicho.go.jp |date=2010-03-31 |accessdate=2022-08-19}}</ref>
*Buddhist objects {{nihongo|''Butsugu''|仏具}} *Clothing and accessories {{nihongo|''Fukushoku''|服飾}} *Furniture {{nihongo|''Chōdo Hin''|調度品}} *Games {{nihongo|''Yūgi gu''|遊戯具}} *Music instruments {{nihongo|''Gakki''|楽器}} *Weaponry {{nihongo|''Buji''|武事}}
==Documents== Generally considered separate from the treasures, the Shōsōin also preserved more than 10,000 documents, mostly from an eighth-century {{ill|scriptorium|en|scriptorium|WD=Q655185|lt=scriptorium|1=Scriptorium}}. These documents contain diverse materials including censuses, tax records, and poetry. The vast majority of documents, however, record the day-to-day record keeping of a sutra copying office active, albeit under a number of different names, between 727–776. The collection is perhaps unparalleled globally as a tightly focused eighth-century archival collection of manuscripts.
The documents were first rediscovered in the 1830s by an antiquarian scholar named Hoida Tadatomo. Hoida and his successors peeled individual sheets apart and reassembled them into new scrolls, disrupting the original organization of the documents. Print versions of most of the documents were published in the first twenty-five volumes of Dai Nihon komonjo beginning in 1901. Subsequent scholarship has allowed scholars to better understand how the scrolls were originally configured. They have also been digitized and are now publicly available for viewing.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/en-US/search/|title=正倉院 - 正倉院|website=shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp}}</ref>
== Silk collection == Since 1994, the Imperial Household Agency's Office of the Shoso-in Treasure House, which is responsible for the administration of the repository, has been producing exact reproduction of ancient Nara textiles. Apart from the appearance and colour, care has been given to reproduce the production and weaving style. The silk is donated each year by [[Empress Masako]], who personally runs the Momijiyama Imperial Cocoonery at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |title=Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress - The Imperial Household Agency |work=Activities of Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress |publisher=Imperial Household Agency |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20230210234113/https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |archivedate=2023-02-10 |accessdate=2023-02-15 }}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|Kura (storehouse)|en|Kura (storehouse)|WD=Q6445494|lt=Kura (storehouse)|1=Kura (storehouse)}} * The {{ill|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|en|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|WD=Q3330314|lt=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|1=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures}} designed to resemble the Shōsō-in, also holds a fine collection of Japanese art and sculpture.
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons Category|Shōsōin}} *[https://shosoin.princeton.edu Guide to Shosoin research (Nara period written documents of the scriptorium)] *[http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/en-US/ Imperial Household Agency page for Shōsōin (in English)] *[http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/ Imperial Household Agency page for Shōsōin (in Japanese)] *[http://reijiyamashina.sakura.ne.jp/shosoin/shosoin.html Shosoin Tanabetsu Mokuroku (List of Shosoin Solid Objects)]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} [[File:Shoso-in.jpg|thumb|300px|Shōsō-in]] The {{nihongo|'''Shōsō-in'''|正倉院}} is the of [[Tōdai-ji]] Temple in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref name="shosoin">{{cite web|title=正倉院ホームページ (Shōsōin Homepage, Imperial Household Agency (Japan))|language= ja|url=http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/|access-date = 2007-03-12}}</ref><ref>"Shōsōin" originally stood for the warehouse area that many of the Buddhist temples and governmental sites in the ages of {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} were known to have, and {{nihongo|"Shōsō"|正倉}} was meaning each independent building located in such an area. However, all but the one in Tōdai-ji were lost over time, thus Shōsōin became a proper noun for the only remaining treasure house building at Tōdai-ji.</ref> The building is in the ''{{ill|Azekura-zukuri|en|Azekura-zukuri|WD=Q2875112|lt=Azekura-zukuri|1=Azekura-zukuri}}'' ([[:en:log-cabin|log-cabin]]) style with a raised floor. It lies to the northwest of the Great Buddha Hall. The Shōsō-in houses artifacts connected to {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} (聖武天皇)(701–756) and {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} (光明皇后)(701–760), as well as arts and crafts of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} (天平) era of {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=Japanese history|1=History of Japan}}.
== History == [[File:Kokka Chinpoh Choh Shosoin.JPG|thumb|Dedicatory records of Tōdai-ji temple, 756]] The construction of the [[Tōdai-ji]] Buddhist temple complex was ordained by {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} as part of a national project of Buddhist temple construction. During the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} period, the years during which {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} reigned, multiple disasters struck Japan as well as political uproar and epidemics. Because of these reasons {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} launched a project of provincial temples.<ref>{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple|1=Provincial temple}}</ref>{{Circular reference|date=April 2018}} The [[Tōdai-ji]] was appointed as the head temple of these provincial temples. {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} was a strong supporter of Buddhism and he thought it would strengthen his central authority as well. The origin of [[Tōdai-ji]]'s Shōsō-in repository itself dates back to 756, when {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} dedicated over 600 items to the Great Buddha at [[Tōdai-ji]] to express her love for her lost husband, Emperor Shōmu, who died 49 days earlier.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408">'Treasures in the Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi. Japan Quarterly, Oct 1, 1970, Vol.17(4), p.408.</ref> Her donation was made over five times across several years, then stored at the Shōsō-in. During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, a large number of treasures, consisting of items and instruments used in important Buddhist services were transferred from a different warehouse called the Kensakuin to the [[Tōdai-ji]].
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it came under the administration of the [[Government of Japan|national government]], and since {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} has been under the administration of the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. It is on the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} register of {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}s as one of the {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|WD=Q1055749|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara}}. It is also a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.
== Building == [[File:Azekura-dukuri JPN.JPG|thumb|''Azekura'' style of architecture on another store house at the Tōdai-ji]] [[File:正倉院 - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|Detail of the Shōsōin]] The building is in the [[:en:Azekurazukuri|Azekura Zukuri]] log-cabin style, with a floor raised to about 2.5 m {{nihongo|''takayuka-shiki''|高床式}}.<ref name="Shosoin 2009">'Shosoin : The oldest archive in Japan.' Library and archival security journal. Volume 22, 2009.</ref> This is an architectural style that was mainly used for the construction of {{ill|Granary|en|Granary|WD=Q114768|lt=granaries|1=Granary}} and storehouses. Some distinctive features of this building style are the triangular, wooden beams that come together in the corners, as well as the fact that it was assembled without using any bolts nor nails. This could be slightly surprising for its height of 14 m, width of 33 m and depth of about 9.3 m.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> However, it was a logical and smart step. As a result of assembling the storehouse without bolts or nails, the structure became very flexible and able to withstand earthquakes, a phenomenon of nature with which Japan was already well acquainted during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. The Shōsō-in is also the only building to survive the {{ill|Siege of Nara|en|Siege of Nara|WD=Q1116528|lt=Siege of Nara|1=Siege of Nara}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name=Turnbull3>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |isbn=0026205408 |page=50}}</ref> The exact construction date is unclear, but construction works probably started soon after the empress's bequest in AD 756 and definitely were finished before AD 759, when the bequest items storage lists were complete.
==Preservation of the artifacts== Since the Shōsō-in was to be a repository for (valuable) objects, it was constructed to create a natural climate regulation system. This natural climate regulation system was created by elevating the floor to a height of 2.7 m.<ref>'Shosoin' Japan Knowledge Lib, encyclopedia of Japan.</ref> This made circulation of air underneath the building possible and protected the structure against humidity at the same time. In addition to this, during the first few decades after its construction, the triangular beams of the Japanese cypress might have functioned as a natural regulator of humidity and temperature. The artefacts themselves were stored away in chests made from {{ill|Cedrus|en|Cedrus|WD=Q128550|lt=cedar|1=Cedrus}} wood, which is known for its durability.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> These chests were 90–110 cm long, 60–70 cm wide and 40–50 cm high. Not only the building itself but also these chests were elevated from the ground. All these adjustments made it possible to preserve the treasures in perfect state.
== Treasures == The Shōsō-in today holds around 9,000 items, excluding items that are yet to be classified.<ref>[https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385379 Piggott, Joan. (1990). "Mokkan. Wooden Documents from the Nara Period"], ''Monumenta Nipponica'', 45:4, pp. 449-470.</ref> The treasures that were donated by Empress Kōmyō were stored in the Hoku Sō, the Northern part of the Shōsōin. From the very beginning, this part of the Shōsō-in has been sealed by the imperial family. One was permitted to enter only with explicit permission of the imperial family.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408"/> Ninety-five percent of the fine arts and crafts in the Shōsōin were produced in Japan around the 8th century, with the remainder imported from the Tang Dynasty, Central Asia, India, Iran, etc. during the same period.<ref name = "narajoshi">[https://megalodon.jp/2020-0304-2055-57/www.nara-wu.ac.jp/liaison/lecture/20160827/ 公開講座『正倉院の工芸 遣唐使は何を持ちかえったか』] Nara Women's University</ref><ref name = "nishikawa">Akihiko Nishikawa. ''正倉院への道天平の至宝'' p.132. Yuzankaku Shuppan. {{ISBN|978-4639016434}}</ref> The designs of these Japanese and East Asian treasures show Iranian, Greek, Roman, and Egyptian influences due to cultural exchange via the Silk Road.<ref name = "narajoshi"/><ref name = "nishikawa"/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Eiland |first=Murray |date=1996 |title=The Ceramics of the Silk Route: Parthia and China |url=https://www.academia.edu/8013347 |journal=Transactions of the Oriental Ceramic Society |volume=60 |pages=105–120}}</ref>
Although these collections are not open to the public, selections are shown at {{ill|Nara National Museum|en|Nara National Museum|WD=Q147312|lt=Nara National Museum|1=Nara National Museum}} once a year in autumn.
The objects and treasures that have been stored in the Shōsō-in can be divided into the following categories. <ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/shisetsu/shosoin01.html |title=The Shosoin Repository - The Imperial Household Agency |publisher=Kunaicho.go.jp |date=2010-03-31 |accessdate=2022-08-19}}</ref>
*Buddhist objects {{nihongo|''Butsugu''|仏具}} *Clothing and accessories {{nihongo|''Fukushoku''|服飾}} *Furniture {{nihongo|''Chōdo Hin''|調度品}} *Games {{nihongo|''Yūgi gu''|遊戯具}} *Music instruments {{nihongo|''Gakki''|楽器}} *Weaponry {{nihongo|''Buji''|武事}}
==Documents== Generally considered separate from the treasures, the Shōsōin also preserved more than 10,000 documents, mostly from an eighth-century {{ill|scriptorium|en|scriptorium|WD=Q655185|lt=scriptorium|1=Scriptorium}}. These documents contain diverse materials including censuses, tax records, and poetry. The vast majority of documents, however, record the day-to-day record keeping of a sutra copying office active, albeit under a number of different names, between 727–776. The collection is perhaps unparalleled globally as a tightly focused eighth-century archival collection of manuscripts.
The documents were first rediscovered in the 1830s by an antiquarian scholar named Hoida Tadatomo. Hoida and his successors peeled individual sheets apart and reassembled them into new scrolls, disrupting the original organization of the documents. Print versions of most of the documents were published in the first twenty-five volumes of Dai Nihon komonjo beginning in 1901. Subsequent scholarship has allowed scholars to better understand how the scrolls were originally configured. They have also been digitized and are now publicly available for viewing.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/en-US/search/|title=正倉院 - 正倉院|website=shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp}}</ref>
== Silk collection == Since 1994, the Imperial Household Agency's Office of the Shoso-in Treasure House, which is responsible for the administration of the repository, has been producing exact reproduction of ancient Nara textiles. Apart from the appearance and colour, care has been given to reproduce the production and weaving style. The silk is donated each year by [[Empress Masako]], who personally runs the Momijiyama Imperial Cocoonery at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |title=Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress - The Imperial Household Agency |work=Activities of Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress |publisher=Imperial Household Agency |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20230210234113/https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |archivedate=2023-02-10 |accessdate=2023-02-15 }}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|Kura (storehouse)|en|Kura (storehouse)|WD=Q6445494|lt=Kura (storehouse)|1=Kura (storehouse)}} * The {{ill|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|en|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|WD=Q3330314|lt=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|1=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures}} designed to resemble the Shōsō-in, also holds a fine collection of Japanese art and sculpture.
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons Category|Shōsōin}} *[https://shosoin.princeton.edu Guide to Shosoin research (Nara period written documents of the scriptorium)] *[http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/en-US/ Imperial Household Agency page for Shōsōin (in English)] *[http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/ Imperial Household Agency page for Shōsōin (in Japanese)] *[http://reijiyamashina.sakura.ne.jp/shosoin/shosoin.html Shosoin Tanabetsu Mokuroku (List of Shosoin Solid Objects)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوسو-إن]][[az:Şosoin]][[de:Shōsō-in]][[en:Shōsōin]][[fa:شوسواین]][[fr:Shōsō-in]][[hy:Սյոսոին]][[it:Shōsō-in]][[ja:正倉院]][[ko:쇼소인]][[nl:Shōsōin]][[pl:Shōsō-in]][[ru:Сёсоин]][[simple:Shōsōin]][[sl:Šoso-in]][[tr:Shōsōin]][[uk:Сьосоїн]][[zh:正仓院]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوسو-إن]][[az:Şosoin]][[de:Shōsō-in]][[en:Shōsōin]][[fa:شوسواین]][[fr:Shōsō-in]][[hy:Սյոսոին]][[it:Shōsō-in]][[ja:正倉院]][[ko:쇼소인]][[nl:Shōsōin]][[pl:Shōsō-in]][[ru:Сёсоин]][[simple:Shōsōin]][[sl:Šoso-in]][[tr:Shōsōin]][[uk:Сьосоїн]][[zh:正仓院]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوسو-إن]][[az:Şosoin]][[de:Shōsō-in]][[en:Shōsōin]][[fa:شوسواین]][[fr:Shōsō-in]][[hy:Սյոսոին]][[it:Shōsō-in]][[ja:正倉院]][[ko:쇼소인]][[nl:Shōsōin]][[pl:Shōsō-in]][[ru:Сёсоин]][[simple:Shōsōin]][[sl:Šoso-in]][[tr:Shōsōin]][[uk:Сьосоїн]][[zh:正仓院]]
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} [[File:Shoso-in.jpg|thumb|300px|Shōsō-in]] The {{nihongo|'''Shōsō-in'''|正倉院}} is the of [[Tōdai-ji]] Temple in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref name="shosoin">{{cite web|title=正倉院ホームページ (Shōsōin Homepage, Imperial Household Agency (Japan))|language= ja|url=http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/|access-date = 2007-03-12}}</ref><ref>"Shōsōin" originally stood for the warehouse area that many of the Buddhist temples and governmental sites in the ages of {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} were known to have, and {{nihongo|"Shōsō"|正倉}} was meaning each independent building located in such an area. However, all but the one in Tōdai-ji were lost over time, thus Shōsōin became a proper noun for the only remaining treasure house building at Tōdai-ji.</ref> The building is in the ''{{ill|Azekura-zukuri|en|Azekura-zukuri|WD=Q2875112|lt=Azekura-zukuri|1=Azekura-zukuri}}'' ([[:en:log-cabin|log-cabin]]) style with a raised floor. It lies to the northwest of the Great Buddha Hall. The Shōsō-in houses artifacts connected to {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} (聖武天皇)(701–756) and {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} (光明皇后)(701–760), as well as arts and crafts of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} (天平) era of {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=Japanese history|1=History of Japan}}.
== History == [[File:Kokka Chinpoh Choh Shosoin.JPG|thumb|Dedicatory records of Tōdai-ji temple, 756]] The construction of the [[Tōdai-ji]] Buddhist temple complex was ordained by {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} as part of a national project of Buddhist temple construction. During the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} period, the years during which {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} reigned, multiple disasters struck Japan as well as political uproar and epidemics. Because of these reasons {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} launched a project of provincial temples.<ref>{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple|1=Provincial temple}}</ref>{{Circular reference|date=April 2018}} The [[Tōdai-ji]] was appointed as the head temple of these provincial temples. {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} was a strong supporter of Buddhism and he thought it would strengthen his central authority as well. The origin of [[Tōdai-ji]]'s Shōsō-in repository itself dates back to 756, when {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} dedicated over 600 items to the Great Buddha at [[Tōdai-ji]] to express her love for her lost husband, Emperor Shōmu, who died 49 days earlier.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408">'Treasures in the Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi. Japan Quarterly, Oct 1, 1970, Vol.17(4), p.408.</ref> Her donation was made over five times across several years, then stored at the Shōsō-in. During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, a large number of treasures, consisting of items and instruments used in important Buddhist services were transferred from a different warehouse called the Kensakuin to the [[Tōdai-ji]].
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it came under the administration of the [[Government of Japan|national government]], and since {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} has been under the administration of the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. It is on the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} register of {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}s as one of the {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|WD=Q1055749|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara}}. It is also a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.
== Building == [[File:Azekura-dukuri JPN.JPG|thumb|''Azekura'' style of architecture on another store house at the Tōdai-ji]] [[File:正倉院 - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|Detail of the Shōsōin]] The building is in the [[:en:Azekurazukuri|Azekura Zukuri]] log-cabin style, with a floor raised to about 2.5 m {{nihongo|''takayuka-shiki''|高床式}}.<ref name="Shosoin 2009">'Shosoin : The oldest archive in Japan.' Library and archival security journal. Volume 22, 2009.</ref> This is an architectural style that was mainly used for the construction of {{ill|Granary|en|Granary|WD=Q114768|lt=granaries|1=Granary}} and storehouses. Some distinctive features of this building style are the triangular, wooden beams that come together in the corners, as well as the fact that it was assembled without using any bolts nor nails. This could be slightly surprising for its height of 14 m, width of 33 m and depth of about 9.3 m.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> However, it was a logical and smart step. As a result of assembling the storehouse without bolts or nails, the structure became very flexible and able to withstand earthquakes, a phenomenon of nature with which Japan was already well acquainted during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. The Shōsō-in is also the only building to survive the {{ill|Siege of Nara|en|Siege of Nara|WD=Q1116528|lt=Siege of Nara|1=Siege of Nara}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name=Turnbull3>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |isbn=0026205408 |page=50}}</ref> The exact construction date is unclear, but construction works probably started soon after the empress's bequest in AD 756 and definitely were finished before AD 759, when the bequest items storage lists were complete.
==Preservation of the artifacts== Since the Shōsō-in was to be a repository for (valuable) objects, it was constructed to create a natural climate regulation system. This natural climate regulation system was created by elevating the floor to a height of 2.7 m.<ref>'Shosoin' Japan Knowledge Lib, encyclopedia of Japan.</ref> This made circulation of air underneath the building possible and protected the structure against humidity at the same time. In addition to this, during the first few decades after its construction, the triangular beams of the Japanese cypress might have functioned as a natural regulator of humidity and temperature. The artefacts themselves were stored away in chests made from {{ill|Cedrus|en|Cedrus|WD=Q128550|lt=cedar|1=Cedrus}} wood, which is known for its durability.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> These chests were 90–110 cm long, 60–70 cm wide and 40–50 cm high. Not only the building itself but also these chests were elevated from the ground. All these adjustments made it possible to preserve the treasures in perfect state.
== Treasures == The Shōsō-in today holds around 9,000 items, excluding items that are yet to be classified.<ref>[https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385379 Piggott, Joan. (1990). "Mokkan. Wooden Documents from the Nara Period"], ''Monumenta Nipponica'', 45:4, pp. 449-470.</ref> The treasures that were donated by Empress Kōmyō were stored in the Hoku Sō, the Northern part of the Shōsōin. From the very beginning, this part of the Shōsō-in has been sealed by the imperial family. One was permitted to enter only with explicit permission of the imperial family.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408"/> Ninety-five percent of the fine arts and crafts in the Shōsōin were produced in Japan around the 8th century, with the remainder imported from the Tang Dynasty, Central Asia, India, Iran, etc. during the same period.<ref name = "narajoshi">[https://megalodon.jp/2020-0304-2055-57/www.nara-wu.ac.jp/liaison/lecture/20160827/ 公開講座『正倉院の工芸 遣唐使は何を持ちかえったか』] Nara Women's University</ref><ref name = "nishikawa">Akihiko Nishikawa. ''正倉院への道天平の至宝'' p.132. Yuzankaku Shuppan. {{ISBN|978-4639016434}}</ref> The designs of these Japanese and East Asian treasures show Iranian, Greek, Roman, and Egyptian influences due to cultural exchange via the Silk Road.<ref name = "narajoshi"/><ref name = "nishikawa"/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Eiland |first=Murray |date=1996 |title=The Ceramics of the Silk Route: Parthia and China |url=https://www.academia.edu/8013347 |journal=Transactions of the Oriental Ceramic Society |volume=60 |pages=105–120}}</ref>
Although these collections are not open to the public, selections are shown at {{ill|Nara National Museum|en|Nara National Museum|WD=Q147312|lt=Nara National Museum|1=Nara National Museum}} once a year in autumn.
The objects and treasures that have been stored in the Shōsō-in can be divided into the following categories. <ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/shisetsu/shosoin01.html |title=The Shosoin Repository - The Imperial Household Agency |publisher=Kunaicho.go.jp |date=2010-03-31 |accessdate=2022-08-19}}</ref>
*Buddhist objects {{nihongo|''Butsugu''|仏具}} *Clothing and accessories {{nihongo|''Fukushoku''|服飾}} *Furniture {{nihongo|''Chōdo Hin''|調度品}} *Games {{nihongo|''Yūgi gu''|遊戯具}} *Music instruments {{nihongo|''Gakki''|楽器}} *Weaponry {{nihongo|''Buji''|武事}}
==Documents== Generally considered separate from the treasures, the Shōsōin also preserved more than 10,000 documents, mostly from an eighth-century {{ill|scriptorium|en|scriptorium|WD=Q655185|lt=scriptorium|1=Scriptorium}}. These documents contain diverse materials including censuses, tax records, and poetry. The vast majority of documents, however, record the day-to-day record keeping of a sutra copying office active, albeit under a number of different names, between 727–776. The collection is perhaps unparalleled globally as a tightly focused eighth-century archival collection of manuscripts.
The documents were first rediscovered in the 1830s by an antiquarian scholar named Hoida Tadatomo. Hoida and his successors peeled individual sheets apart and reassembled them into new scrolls, disrupting the original organization of the documents. Print versions of most of the documents were published in the first twenty-five volumes of Dai Nihon komonjo beginning in 1901. Subsequent scholarship has allowed scholars to better understand how the scrolls were originally configured. They have also been digitized and are now publicly available for viewing.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/en-US/search/|title=正倉院 - 正倉院|website=shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp}}</ref>
== Silk collection == Since 1994, the Imperial Household Agency's Office of the Shoso-in Treasure House, which is responsible for the administration of the repository, has been producing exact reproduction of ancient Nara textiles. Apart from the appearance and colour, care has been given to reproduce the production and weaving style. The silk is donated each year by [[Empress Masako]], who personally runs the Momijiyama Imperial Cocoonery at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |title=Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress - The Imperial Household Agency |work=Activities of Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress |publisher=Imperial Household Agency |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20230210234113/https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |archivedate=2023-02-10 |accessdate=2023-02-15 }}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|Kura (storehouse)|en|Kura (storehouse)|WD=Q6445494|lt=Kura (storehouse)|1=Kura (storehouse)}} * The {{ill|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|en|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|WD=Q3330314|lt=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|1=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures}} designed to resemble the Shōsō-in, also holds a fine collection of Japanese art and sculpture.
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons Category|Shōsōin}} *[https://shosoin.princeton.edu Guide to Shosoin research (Nara period written documents of the scriptorium)] *[http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/en-US/ Imperial Household Agency page for Shōsōin (in English)] *[http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/ Imperial Household Agency page for Shōsōin (in Japanese)] *[http://reijiyamashina.sakura.ne.jp/shosoin/shosoin.html Shosoin Tanabetsu Mokuroku (List of Shosoin Solid Objects)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوسو-إن]][[az:Şosoin]][[de:Shōsō-in]][[en:Shōsōin]][[fa:شوسواین]][[fr:Shōsō-in]][[hy:Սյոսոին]][[it:Shōsō-in]][[ja:正倉院]][[ko:쇼소인]][[nl:Shōsōin]][[pl:Shōsō-in]][[ru:Сёсоин]][[simple:Shōsōin]][[sl:Šoso-in]][[tr:Shōsōin]][[uk:Сьосоїн]][[zh:正仓院]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوسو-إن]][[az:Şosoin]][[de:Shōsō-in]][[en:Shōsōin]][[fa:شوسواین]][[fr:Shōsō-in]][[hy:Սյոսոին]][[it:Shōsō-in]][[ja:正倉院]][[ko:쇼소인]][[nl:Shōsōin]][[pl:Shōsō-in]][[ru:Сёсоин]][[simple:Shōsōin]][[sl:Šoso-in]][[tr:Shōsōin]][[uk:Сьосоїн]][[zh:正仓院]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوسو-إن]][[az:Şosoin]][[de:Shōsō-in]][[en:Shōsōin]][[fa:شوسواین]][[fr:Shōsō-in]][[hy:Սյոսոին]][[it:Shōsō-in]][[ja:正倉院]][[ko:쇼소인]][[nl:Shōsōin]][[pl:Shōsō-in]][[ru:Сёсоин]][[simple:Shōsōin]][[sl:Šoso-in]][[tr:Shōsōin]][[uk:Сьосоїн]][[zh:正仓院]]
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} [[File:Shoso-in.jpg|thumb|300px|Shōsō-in]] The {{nihongo|'''Shōsō-in'''|正倉院}} is the of [[Tōdai-ji]] Temple in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref name="shosoin">{{cite web|title=正倉院ホームページ (Shōsōin Homepage, Imperial Household Agency (Japan))|language= ja|url=http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/|access-date = 2007-03-12}}</ref><ref>"Shōsōin" originally stood for the warehouse area that many of the Buddhist temples and governmental sites in the ages of {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} were known to have, and {{nihongo|"Shōsō"|正倉}} was meaning each independent building located in such an area. However, all but the one in Tōdai-ji were lost over time, thus Shōsōin became a proper noun for the only remaining treasure house building at Tōdai-ji.</ref> The building is in the ''{{ill|Azekura-zukuri|en|Azekura-zukuri|WD=Q2875112|lt=Azekura-zukuri|1=Azekura-zukuri}}'' ([[:en:log-cabin|log-cabin]]) style with a raised floor. It lies to the northwest of the Great Buddha Hall. The Shōsō-in houses artifacts connected to {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} (聖武天皇)(701–756) and {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} (光明皇后)(701–760), as well as arts and crafts of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} (天平) era of {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=Japanese history|1=History of Japan}}.
== History == [[File:Kokka Chinpoh Choh Shosoin.JPG|thumb|Dedicatory records of Tōdai-ji temple, 756]] The construction of the [[Tōdai-ji]] Buddhist temple complex was ordained by {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} as part of a national project of Buddhist temple construction. During the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} period, the years during which {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} reigned, multiple disasters struck Japan as well as political uproar and epidemics. Because of these reasons {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} launched a project of provincial temples.<ref>{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple|1=Provincial temple}}</ref>{{Circular reference|date=April 2018}} The [[Tōdai-ji]] was appointed as the head temple of these provincial temples. {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} was a strong supporter of Buddhism and he thought it would strengthen his central authority as well. The origin of [[Tōdai-ji]]'s Shōsō-in repository itself dates back to 756, when {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} dedicated over 600 items to the Great Buddha at [[Tōdai-ji]] to express her love for her lost husband, Emperor Shōmu, who died 49 days earlier.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408">'Treasures in the Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi. Japan Quarterly, Oct 1, 1970, Vol.17(4), p.408.</ref> Her donation was made over five times across several years, then stored at the Shōsō-in. During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, a large number of treasures, consisting of items and instruments used in important Buddhist services were transferred from a different warehouse called the Kensakuin to the [[Tōdai-ji]].
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it came under the administration of the [[Government of Japan|national government]], and since {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} has been under the administration of the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. It is on the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} register of {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}s as one of the {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|WD=Q1055749|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara}}. It is also a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.
== Building == [[File:Azekura-dukuri JPN.JPG|thumb|''Azekura'' style of architecture on another store house at the Tōdai-ji]] [[File:正倉院 - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|Detail of the Shōsōin]] The building is in the [[:en:Azekurazukuri|Azekura Zukuri]] log-cabin style, with a floor raised to about 2.5 m {{nihongo|''takayuka-shiki''|高床式}}.<ref name="Shosoin 2009">'Shosoin : The oldest archive in Japan.' Library and archival security journal. Volume 22, 2009.</ref> This is an architectural style that was mainly used for the construction of {{ill|Granary|en|Granary|WD=Q114768|lt=granaries|1=Granary}} and storehouses. Some distinctive features of this building style are the triangular, wooden beams that come together in the corners, as well as the fact that it was assembled without using any bolts nor nails. This could be slightly surprising for its height of 14 m, width of 33 m and depth of about 9.3 m.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> However, it was a logical and smart step. As a result of assembling the storehouse without bolts or nails, the structure became very flexible and able to withstand earthquakes, a phenomenon of nature with which Japan was already well acquainted during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. The Shōsō-in is also the only building to survive the {{ill|Siege of Nara|en|Siege of Nara|WD=Q1116528|lt=Siege of Nara|1=Siege of Nara}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name=Turnbull3>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |isbn=0026205408 |page=50}}</ref> The exact construction date is unclear, but construction works probably started soon after the empress's bequest in AD 756 and definitely were finished before AD 759, when the bequest items storage lists were complete.
==Preservation of the artifacts== Since the Shōsō-in was to be a repository for (valuable) objects, it was constructed to create a natural climate regulation system. This natural climate regulation system was created by elevating the floor to a height of 2.7 m.<ref>'Shosoin' Japan Knowledge Lib, encyclopedia of Japan.</ref> This made circulation of air underneath the building possible and protected the structure against humidity at the same time. In addition to this, during the first few decades after its construction, the triangular beams of the Japanese cypress might have functioned as a natural regulator of humidity and temperature. The artefacts themselves were stored away in chests made from {{ill|Cedrus|en|Cedrus|WD=Q128550|lt=cedar|1=Cedrus}} wood, which is known for its durability.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> These chests were 90–110 cm long, 60–70 cm wide and 40–50 cm high. Not only the building itself but also these chests were elevated from the ground. All these adjustments made it possible to preserve the treasures in perfect state.
== Treasures == The Shōsō-in today holds around 9,000 items, excluding items that are yet to be classified.<ref>[https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385379 Piggott, Joan. (1990). "Mokkan. Wooden Documents from the Nara Period"], ''Monumenta Nipponica'', 45:4, pp. 449-470.</ref> The treasures that were donated by Empress Kōmyō were stored in the Hoku Sō, the Northern part of the Shōsōin. From the very beginning, this part of the Shōsō-in has been sealed by the imperial family. One was permitted to enter only with explicit permission of the imperial family.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408"/> Ninety-five percent of the fine arts and crafts in the Shōsōin were produced in Japan around the 8th century, with the remainder imported from the Tang Dynasty, Central Asia, India, Iran, etc. during the same period.<ref name = "narajoshi">[https://megalodon.jp/2020-0304-2055-57/www.nara-wu.ac.jp/liaison/lecture/20160827/ 公開講座『正倉院の工芸 遣唐使は何を持ちかえったか』] Nara Women's University</ref><ref name = "nishikawa">Akihiko Nishikawa. ''正倉院への道天平の至宝'' p.132. Yuzankaku Shuppan. {{ISBN|978-4639016434}}</ref> The designs of these Japanese and East Asian treasures show Iranian, Greek, Roman, and Egyptian influences due to cultural exchange via the Silk Road.<ref name = "narajoshi"/><ref name = "nishikawa"/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Eiland |first=Murray |date=1996 |title=The Ceramics of the Silk Route: Parthia and China |url=https://www.academia.edu/8013347 |journal=Transactions of the Oriental Ceramic Society |volume=60 |pages=105–120}}</ref>
Although these collections are not open to the public, selections are shown at {{ill|Nara National Museum|en|Nara National Museum|WD=Q147312|lt=Nara National Museum|1=Nara National Museum}} once a year in autumn.
The objects and treasures that have been stored in the Shōsō-in can be divided into the following categories. <ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/shisetsu/shosoin01.html |title=The Shosoin Repository - The Imperial Household Agency |publisher=Kunaicho.go.jp |date=2010-03-31 |accessdate=2022-08-19}}</ref>
*Buddhist objects {{nihongo|''Butsugu''|仏具}} *Clothing and accessories {{nihongo|''Fukushoku''|服飾}} *Furniture {{nihongo|''Chōdo Hin''|調度品}} *Games {{nihongo|''Yūgi gu''|遊戯具}} *Music instruments {{nihongo|''Gakki''|楽器}} *Weaponry {{nihongo|''Buji''|武事}}
==Documents== Generally considered separate from the treasures, the Shōsōin also preserved more than 10,000 documents, mostly from an eighth-century {{ill|scriptorium|en|scriptorium|WD=Q655185|lt=scriptorium|1=Scriptorium}}. These documents contain diverse materials including censuses, tax records, and poetry. The vast majority of documents, however, record the day-to-day record keeping of a sutra copying office active, albeit under a number of different names, between 727–776. The collection is perhaps unparalleled globally as a tightly focused eighth-century archival collection of manuscripts.
The documents were first rediscovered in the 1830s by an antiquarian scholar named Hoida Tadatomo. Hoida and his successors peeled individual sheets apart and reassembled them into new scrolls, disrupting the original organization of the documents. Print versions of most of the documents were published in the first twenty-five volumes of Dai Nihon komonjo beginning in 1901. Subsequent scholarship has allowed scholars to better understand how the scrolls were originally configured. They have also been digitized and are now publicly available for viewing.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/en-US/search/|title=正倉院 - 正倉院|website=shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp}}</ref>
== Silk collection == Since 1994, the Imperial Household Agency's Office of the Shoso-in Treasure House, which is responsible for the administration of the repository, has been producing exact reproduction of ancient Nara textiles. Apart from the appearance and colour, care has been given to reproduce the production and weaving style. The silk is donated each year by [[Empress Masako]], who personally runs the Momijiyama Imperial Cocoonery at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |title=Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress - The Imperial Household Agency |work=Activities of Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress |publisher=Imperial Household Agency |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20230210234113/https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |archivedate=2023-02-10 |accessdate=2023-02-15 }}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|Kura (storehouse)|en|Kura (storehouse)|WD=Q6445494|lt=Kura (storehouse)|1=Kura (storehouse)}} * The {{ill|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|en|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|WD=Q3330314|lt=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|1=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures}} designed to resemble the Shōsō-in, also holds a fine collection of Japanese art and sculpture.
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons Category|Shōsōin}} *[https://shosoin.princeton.edu Guide to Shosoin research (Nara period written documents of the scriptorium)] *[http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/en-US/ Imperial Household Agency page for Shōsōin (in English)] *[http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/ Imperial Household Agency page for Shōsōin (in Japanese)] *[http://reijiyamashina.sakura.ne.jp/shosoin/shosoin.html Shosoin Tanabetsu Mokuroku (List of Shosoin Solid Objects)]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} [[File:Shoso-in.jpg|thumb|300px|Shōsō-in]] The {{nihongo|'''Shōsō-in'''|正倉院}} is the of [[Tōdai-ji]] Temple in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref name="shosoin">{{cite web|title=正倉院ホームページ (Shōsōin Homepage, Imperial Household Agency (Japan))|language= ja|url=http://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/|access-date = 2007-03-12}}</ref><ref>"Shōsōin" originally stood for the warehouse area that many of the Buddhist temples and governmental sites in the ages of {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} were known to have, and {{nihongo|"Shōsō"|正倉}} was meaning each independent building located in such an area. However, all but the one in Tōdai-ji were lost over time, thus Shōsōin became a proper noun for the only remaining treasure house building at Tōdai-ji.</ref> The building is in the ''{{ill|Azekura-zukuri|en|Azekura-zukuri|WD=Q2875112|lt=Azekura-zukuri|1=Azekura-zukuri}}'' ([[:en:log-cabin|log-cabin]]) style with a raised floor. It lies to the northwest of the Great Buddha Hall. The Shōsō-in houses artifacts connected to {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} (聖武天皇)(701–756) and {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} (光明皇后)(701–760), as well as arts and crafts of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} (天平) era of {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=Japanese history|1=History of Japan}}.
== History == [[File:Kokka Chinpoh Choh Shosoin.JPG|thumb|Dedicatory records of Tōdai-ji temple, 756]] The construction of the [[Tōdai-ji]] Buddhist temple complex was ordained by {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} as part of a national project of Buddhist temple construction. During the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tempyō|1=Tenpyō}} period, the years during which {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} reigned, multiple disasters struck Japan as well as political uproar and epidemics. Because of these reasons {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} launched a project of provincial temples.<ref>{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple|1=Provincial temple}}</ref>{{Circular reference|date=April 2018}} The [[Tōdai-ji]] was appointed as the head temple of these provincial temples. {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} was a strong supporter of Buddhism and he thought it would strengthen his central authority as well. The origin of [[Tōdai-ji]]'s Shōsō-in repository itself dates back to 756, when {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|WD=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} dedicated over 600 items to the Great Buddha at [[Tōdai-ji]] to express her love for her lost husband, Emperor Shōmu, who died 49 days earlier.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408">'Treasures in the Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi. Japan Quarterly, Oct 1, 1970, Vol.17(4), p.408.</ref> Her donation was made over five times across several years, then stored at the Shōsō-in. During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, a large number of treasures, consisting of items and instruments used in important Buddhist services were transferred from a different warehouse called the Kensakuin to the [[Tōdai-ji]].
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it came under the administration of the [[Government of Japan|national government]], and since {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} has been under the administration of the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. It is on the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} register of {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}s as one of the {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|WD=Q1055749|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara}}. It is also a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.
== Building == [[File:Azekura-dukuri JPN.JPG|thumb|''Azekura'' style of architecture on another store house at the Tōdai-ji]] [[File:正倉院 - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|Detail of the Shōsōin]] The building is in the [[:en:Azekurazukuri|Azekura Zukuri]] log-cabin style, with a floor raised to about 2.5 m {{nihongo|''takayuka-shiki''|高床式}}.<ref name="Shosoin 2009">'Shosoin : The oldest archive in Japan.' Library and archival security journal. Volume 22, 2009.</ref> This is an architectural style that was mainly used for the construction of {{ill|Granary|en|Granary|WD=Q114768|lt=granaries|1=Granary}} and storehouses. Some distinctive features of this building style are the triangular, wooden beams that come together in the corners, as well as the fact that it was assembled without using any bolts nor nails. This could be slightly surprising for its height of 14 m, width of 33 m and depth of about 9.3 m.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> However, it was a logical and smart step. As a result of assembling the storehouse without bolts or nails, the structure became very flexible and able to withstand earthquakes, a phenomenon of nature with which Japan was already well acquainted during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. The Shōsō-in is also the only building to survive the {{ill|Siege of Nara|en|Siege of Nara|WD=Q1116528|lt=Siege of Nara|1=Siege of Nara}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name=Turnbull3>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |isbn=0026205408 |page=50}}</ref> The exact construction date is unclear, but construction works probably started soon after the empress's bequest in AD 756 and definitely were finished before AD 759, when the bequest items storage lists were complete.
==Preservation of the artifacts== Since the Shōsō-in was to be a repository for (valuable) objects, it was constructed to create a natural climate regulation system. This natural climate regulation system was created by elevating the floor to a height of 2.7 m.<ref>'Shosoin' Japan Knowledge Lib, encyclopedia of Japan.</ref> This made circulation of air underneath the building possible and protected the structure against humidity at the same time. In addition to this, during the first few decades after its construction, the triangular beams of the Japanese cypress might have functioned as a natural regulator of humidity and temperature. The artefacts themselves were stored away in chests made from {{ill|Cedrus|en|Cedrus|WD=Q128550|lt=cedar|1=Cedrus}} wood, which is known for its durability.<ref name="Shosoin 2009"/> These chests were 90–110 cm long, 60–70 cm wide and 40–50 cm high. Not only the building itself but also these chests were elevated from the ground. All these adjustments made it possible to preserve the treasures in perfect state.
== Treasures == The Shōsō-in today holds around 9,000 items, excluding items that are yet to be classified.<ref>[https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385379 Piggott, Joan. (1990). "Mokkan. Wooden Documents from the Nara Period"], ''Monumenta Nipponica'', 45:4, pp. 449-470.</ref> The treasures that were donated by Empress Kōmyō were stored in the Hoku Sō, the Northern part of the Shōsōin. From the very beginning, this part of the Shōsō-in has been sealed by the imperial family. One was permitted to enter only with explicit permission of the imperial family.<ref name="Shousouin' Mizuo Hiroshi 1970, p.408"/> Ninety-five percent of the fine arts and crafts in the Shōsōin were produced in Japan around the 8th century, with the remainder imported from the Tang Dynasty, Central Asia, India, Iran, etc. during the same period.<ref name = "narajoshi">[https://megalodon.jp/2020-0304-2055-57/www.nara-wu.ac.jp/liaison/lecture/20160827/ 公開講座『正倉院の工芸 遣唐使は何を持ちかえったか』] Nara Women's University</ref><ref name = "nishikawa">Akihiko Nishikawa. ''正倉院への道天平の至宝'' p.132. Yuzankaku Shuppan. {{ISBN|978-4639016434}}</ref> The designs of these Japanese and East Asian treasures show Iranian, Greek, Roman, and Egyptian influences due to cultural exchange via the Silk Road.<ref name = "narajoshi"/><ref name = "nishikawa"/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Eiland |first=Murray |date=1996 |title=The Ceramics of the Silk Route: Parthia and China |url=https://www.academia.edu/8013347 |journal=Transactions of the Oriental Ceramic Society |volume=60 |pages=105–120}}</ref>
Although these collections are not open to the public, selections are shown at {{ill|Nara National Museum|en|Nara National Museum|WD=Q147312|lt=Nara National Museum|1=Nara National Museum}} once a year in autumn.
The objects and treasures that have been stored in the Shōsō-in can be divided into the following categories. <ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/shisetsu/shosoin01.html |title=The Shosoin Repository - The Imperial Household Agency |publisher=Kunaicho.go.jp |date=2010-03-31 |accessdate=2022-08-19}}</ref>
*Buddhist objects {{nihongo|''Butsugu''|仏具}} *Clothing and accessories {{nihongo|''Fukushoku''|服飾}} *Furniture {{nihongo|''Chōdo Hin''|調度品}} *Games {{nihongo|''Yūgi gu''|遊戯具}} *Music instruments {{nihongo|''Gakki''|楽器}} *Weaponry {{nihongo|''Buji''|武事}}
==Documents== Generally considered separate from the treasures, the Shōsōin also preserved more than 10,000 documents, mostly from an eighth-century {{ill|scriptorium|en|scriptorium|WD=Q655185|lt=scriptorium|1=Scriptorium}}. These documents contain diverse materials including censuses, tax records, and poetry. The vast majority of documents, however, record the day-to-day record keeping of a sutra copying office active, albeit under a number of different names, between 727–776. The collection is perhaps unparalleled globally as a tightly focused eighth-century archival collection of manuscripts.
The documents were first rediscovered in the 1830s by an antiquarian scholar named Hoida Tadatomo. Hoida and his successors peeled individual sheets apart and reassembled them into new scrolls, disrupting the original organization of the documents. Print versions of most of the documents were published in the first twenty-five volumes of Dai Nihon komonjo beginning in 1901. Subsequent scholarship has allowed scholars to better understand how the scrolls were originally configured. They have also been digitized and are now publicly available for viewing.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp/en-US/search/|title=正倉院 - 正倉院|website=shosoin.kunaicho.go.jp}}</ref>
== Silk collection == Since 1994, the Imperial Household Agency's Office of the Shoso-in Treasure House, which is responsible for the administration of the repository, has been producing exact reproduction of ancient Nara textiles. Apart from the appearance and colour, care has been given to reproduce the production and weaving style. The silk is donated each year by [[Empress Masako]], who personally runs the Momijiyama Imperial Cocoonery at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |title=Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress - The Imperial Household Agency |work=Activities of Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress |publisher=Imperial Household Agency |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20230210234113/https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02.html |archivedate=2023-02-10 |accessdate=2023-02-15 }}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|Kura (storehouse)|en|Kura (storehouse)|WD=Q6445494|lt=Kura (storehouse)|1=Kura (storehouse)}} * The {{ill|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|en|Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|WD=Q3330314|lt=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures|1=Kamakura Museum of National Treasures}} designed to resemble the Shōsō-in, also holds a fine collection of Japanese art and sculpture.
